ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general barley beverage class; no specific entry for packaged less-sugar variant. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers barley-based beverages broadly; no specific less-sugar packaged sub-code exists. Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged barley drink; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve portion is one 250 ml can or bottle, equivalent to approximately 250 g, commonly consumed as a chilled ready-to-drink beverage in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Barley drink (less sugar, packaged) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo database for 'Barley drink (less sugar, packaged)' with cr_id D05010004. All per-100g values taken directly from this source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — sugar-sweetened beverages and barley-based drinks analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for packaged less-sugar barley drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the closest analogous entry for lightly sweetened barley-based beverages and sugar-sweetened soft drinks (GI range 55–65 per University of Sydney GI Database). Carbohydrate per serving = 17.5 g (7.0 g/100g × 250 g serving); GL = (55 × 17.5) / 100 = 9.6. This is a conservative analogous estimate; actual GI may vary by formulation. GI 55 is classified as low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (6.0 g sugar/100 g) exceeding the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar gate; 'less sugar' labelling does not remove SSB classification
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage as its 6 g/100 g sugar content classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage, which independently raises blood pressure risk regardless of its low sodium content (2 mg/100 g); choose genuinely unsweetened barley water (0 g sugar) instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6 g sugar/100 g, zero dietary fibre, and low GI (~55); liquid sugar absorbs rapidly and elevates postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this packaged barley drink because its 6 g/100 g sugar content and zero fibre cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation; 'less sugar' and 'reduced sugar' variants still qualify as SSBs and are unsuitable for diabetes management — plain water or unsweetened barley water are appropriate alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased cardiovascular risk via triglyceride elevation and insulin resistance; fat and sodium are negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While this beverage contains negligible fat (0 g) and very low sodium (2 mg/100 g), its 6 g/100 g sugar content classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage, and habitual SSB consumption is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular disease risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; limit intake and prefer unsweetened alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6 g/100 g), zero fibre, and absence of any anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as its added sugar content promotes systemic inflammation and it provides no anti-inflammatory benefit; choose green tea, turmeric tea, or plain water for RA-appropriate hydration.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Barley Drink (Less Sugar, Packaged) is a ready-to-drink (RTD) beverage popular in Singapore and across Chinese-influenced Southeast Asian communities. It is made from cooked barley extract diluted in water with a reduced quantity of added sugar, then packaged in cans or bottles for commercial sale. The drink has a mild, slightly sweet, and lightly grainy flavour with a thin, watery texture — distinctly lighter than whole-grain barley preparations. It is commonly consumed chilled as a refreshing everyday beverage, particularly during warm weather, and is widely available in convenience stores, supermarkets, and hawker centres across Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 28 kcal, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, 7 g carbohydrate (of which 6 g is sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and 2 mg sodium. Despite its 'less sugar' labelling, it remains a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) by Singapore HPB and international dietary standards because its sugar content (6 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage threshold. It is therefore unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis. The barley extract in packaged form provides negligible beta-glucan compared to whole barley grain, so the fibre and cholesterol-lowering benefits associated with whole barley do not apply. Clinically, liquid sugar in any quantity is absorbed rapidly and elevates postprandial blood glucose. Individuals seeking a healthier alternative should choose genuinely unsweetened barley water (0 g sugar) or plain water.
common_user_queries: Is barley drink less sugar healthy?
Can diabetics drink less sugar barley drink?
Is packaged barley drink suitable for hypertension?
How many calories in a can of less sugar barley drink?

Is barley water good for blood pressure?
Can I drink barley drink if I have diabetes?
Is less sugar barley drink vegan?

confidence_reason: Exact match found in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010004) with confidence 0.95 assigned by HPB. All per-100g nutrient values are taken directly from this approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the only approved GI source, and is clearly documented as such.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this is a dilute barley-extract beverage with no protein by formulation. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — no fat-containing ingredients are present in this formulation. saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero by formulation (no fat source). dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB — the barley extract in packaged RTD form retains negligible fibre compared to whole barley grain. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific packaged less-sugar barley drink are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D05010004 as of 2026-04-16. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on lightly sweetened barley-based and sugar-sweetened beverages; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product. GL per serving calculated as (55 × 17.5 g carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 9.6. 'Less sugar' labelling does not remove SSB classification under HPB or international dietary guidelines when sugar exceeds 5 g/100 g. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest parent matches; no exact ontology entry exists for packaged less-sugar barley drink.
